front: What is the primary goal of the End-to-End Principle in network design?
back: The End-to-End Principle states that application-specific functions should be implemented in the end hosts of a network rather than in intermediary nodes (like routers), whenever possible. This keeps the core network simple and general-purpose, pushing complexity to the edges.

front: What are the two main approaches to packet switching?
back: 1. Virtual Circuit (VC) Switching: A connection-oriented approach where a fixed path (virtual circuit) is established before data transfer. Packets carry a VC identifier and follow the same path. 2. Datagram (Connectionless) Switching: Each packet is routed independently based on its destination address, and may take different paths to the destination.

A condition where a network becomes so congested that little to no useful communication occurs. It happens when the network is overloaded with retransmitted packets, which in turn cause more congestion and retransmissions, leading to a severe drop in useful throughput.

front: What is the key difference between a router and a switch?
back: A switch (Layer 2/Data Link) forwards frames within a single local area network (LAN) based on MAC addresses. A router (Layer 3/Network) forwards packets between different networks based on IP addresses. Routers connect LANs to form an internetwork.

front: What are the two fundamental functions of a reliable, connection-oriented protocol?
back: 1. Ordered Delivery: Ensuring packets are delivered to the application in the same order they were sent. 2. Error Control: Using acknowledgements (ACKs) and retransmissions to guarantee that all data is delivered correctly and without loss.
